title: Tobacco Use Project
text:
The tobacco and nicotine product landscape is continuously evolving as new products are developed, advertised, and sold to consumers. Tobacco and nicotine products - including cigarettes, electronic cigarettes (e-cigarettes), cigars, smokeless tobacco, and pipes - pose varying health risks to consumers, including lung and oral cancers, cardiovascular risks, and oral health risks.
The last decade has seen many changes in the use of different tobacco products. Sales of e-cigarettes increased substantially beginning in 2017, especially among young adults. Although promoted as a harm reduction and cigarette cessation tool, there remain concerns that these products may appeal to those that may not otherwise use tobacco or nicotine products, or lead to subsequent cigarette use. More recently, beginning around 2021 and 2022, sales of nicotine pouches (a type of smokeless tobacco product) increased. Different sociodemographic groups of the population may use tobacco and nicotine products at varying rates, leading to disparities in health outcomes by sex, race, and age group.
The National Health Interview Survey (NHIS), conducted annually by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS) and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), is a nationally representative, cross-sectional survey on the U.S. civilian non-institutionalized population. Using a complex survey design, NHIS collects data on many health behaviors, including measures of tobacco use among different subgroups of the population. Since NHIS is nationally representative, it can be used to estimate prevalence of tobacco and nicotine product use in the U.S. population as a whole by sociodemographic group. This project aims to assess how the prevalence of tobacco and nicotine product use has changed over time in the U.S. from 2012 to 2022 by sex, race and ethnicity, and age group. Monitoring trends in different tobacco and nicotine product use over time is necessary for understanding the changing public health impacts of these products by sociodemograhpic group and informing public health interventions as tobacco and nicotine products continue to evolve.

title: Tobacco Use Project
text:
The tobacco and nicotine product landscape is continuously evolving as new products are developed, advertised, and sold to consumers. Tobacco and nicotine products - including cigarettes, electronic cigarettes (e-cigarettes), cigars, smokeless tobacco, and pipes - pose varying health risks to consumers, including lung and oral cancers, cardiovascular risks, and oral health risks.
The last decade has seen many changes in the use of different tobacco products. Sales of e-cigarettes increased substantially beginning in 2017, especially among young adults. Although promoted as a harm reduction and cigarette cessation tool, there remain concerns that these products may appeal to those that may not otherwise use tobacco or nicotine products, or lead to subsequent cigarette use. More recently, beginning around 2021 and 2022, sales of nicotine pouches (a type of smokeless tobacco product) increased. Different sociodemographic groups of the population may use tobacco and nicotine products at varying rates, leading to disparities in health outcomes by sex, race, and age group.
The National Health Interview Survey (NHIS), conducted annually by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS) and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), is a nationally representative, cross-sectional survey on the U.S. civilian non-institutionalized population. Using a complex survey design, NHIS collects data on many health behaviors, including measures of tobacco use among different subgroups of the population. Since NHIS is nationally representative, it can be used to estimate prevalence of tobacco and nicotine product use in the U.S. population as a whole by sociodemographic group. This project aims to assess how the prevalence of tobacco and nicotine product use has changed over time in the U.S. from 2012 to 2022 by sex, race and ethnicity, and age group. Monitoring trends in different tobacco and nicotine product use over time is necessary for understanding the changing public health impacts of these products by sociodemograhpic group and informing public health interventions as tobacco and nicotine products continue to evolve.

title: Tobacco Use Project
text:
Methods
NHIS data is publicly available, and can be accessed on the CDC Website. NHIS data from 2012-2022 were obtained from a IPUMS harmonized database.
Five tobacco and nicotine product categories were examined: cigarettes, e-cigarettes, cigars, smokeless tobacco, and pipes. For each product, two use states were defined, derived from tobacco use variables in the dataset. Ever use was defined as respondents who reported ever using the product, while current use was defined as respondents who reported ever use and current use (every day or some days). Current use of cigarettes or cigars only includes participants who used 100 or more cigarettes or cigars in their lifetime, respectively, as is standard in tobacco analyses.
Sociodemographic variables were also defined and categorized. Age groups were categorized into 18-24, 25-34, 35-54, and >55 years. Sex was classified as male or female. Race & Ethnicity was classified into Hispanic, non-Hispanic White, non-Hispanic Black, non-Hispanic Asian, and non-Hispanic Other
The analysis accounted for the complex survey design of NHIS by using survey weights, strata, and primary sampling units. The final dataset included the defined tobacco use variables and was restricted to those 18 years or older with non-missing data on age, sex, race/ethnicity, and survey design variables. The final dataset used for this analysis had 596,023 observations.

title: Tobacco Use Project
text:
Results
The results of this analysis was synthesized into one interactive dashboard of tobacco use trends by sociodemographic group from 2012-2022. These results are nationally representative, since they account for complex survey design.

Trends in Tobacco Use
Overall from 2012-2022, current cigarette use prevalence declined from almost 20% to about 10%. E-cigarette use increased from about 4% to 7%. Cigar use remained relatively steady at about 4%, smokeless tobacco remained at about 2.5%, and pipe remained at about 1-2%.


Differences by Age
Tobacco and nicotine product use trends vary over time by age group. In 2012, individuals 55 years or older were the least likely to use cigarettes, but this prevalence has not declined much over time. Cigarette use for younger age groups had a higher prevalence in 2012 but declined more steeply over time, especially for the 18-24 year old group, which declined from close to 20% to less than 5%. Some of these trends may have been impacted by the use of e-cigarettes, which have been rising in younger age groups.
NHIS began asking about e-cigarettes in 2014. Since 2014, e-cigarette use has increased in every age group, except the 55+ age group, which has seen steady prevalence over time. The prevalence among 18-24-year-olds increased from about 5% in 2014 to almost 14% in 2022. Each subsequently older age group is less likely to use e-cigarettes.
Cigar use is less common with wider confidence intervals, so specific trends are a bit harder to discern. Cigar use trends by age have remained relatively steady over time. Interestingly, the 25-34-year old age group has the highest prevalence of cigar use, followed by 35-54-year olds. 18-24-year olds and 55+ year-olds have similar rates in recent years.
Smokeless tobacco use was the highest for the 18-24-year old age group in 2012 at almost 4%, but this decreased significantly over time. By 2022, 18-24-year olds were the less likely to use smokeless tobacco at about 1%, while other age groups had similar rates over time.
Pipe use decreased in the 18-24 year old age group over time from almost 4% to about 1%, but other groups remained steady over time.


Differences by Sex
Men were consistently more likely to use every type of tobacco and nicotine product than women. Over the entire time period, cigarette use declined for both sexes but remained higher among men than women. There was an analogous increase in e-cigarette use among both sexes that also remained higher among men than women. Cigar use remained relatively steady among both sexes, but men were consistently over five times more likely to use cigars. Men were also more likely to use smokeless tobacco; over 4% of men used smokeless tobacco over the entire period, while less than 0.5% of women used these products. Pipe use prevalence was about 2% in men and 0.5% in women in 2012. By 2022, pipe use in men declined to a bit over 1% in men, but stayed relatively steady in women.


Differences by Race and Ethnicity
Current cigarette use prevalence has declined over time for every race and ethnicity group. Non-Hispanic Asian and Hispanic individuals are less likely to use cigarettes than other groups. Non-Hispanic Other individuals have been the most likely to use cigarettes overall, but the prevalence of use among this age group has declined and in 2022 was similar to use prevalence in non-Hispanic White and non-Hispanic Black individuals. The non-Hispanic other group has less observations and wider confidence intervals, so estimates may be less accurate in this group.
E-cigarette use has increased for every race and ethnicity group, with non-Hispanic Other and non-Hispanic White individuals the most likely to use e-cigarettes. However, there has been a steep increase in e-cigarette use among non-Hispanic black individuals since about 2020.
Non-Hispanic Black individuals are the most likely to use cigars, at over 6% in recent years. Non-hispanic Asian individuals are the least likely to use cigars, followed by Hispanic individuals.
Non-Hispanic White and non-Hispanic other individuals are more than twice as likely to use smokeless tobacco than other race and ethnicity groups (2-3% compared to less than 1%).
Differences in pipe use prevalence by race and ethnicty are a bit harder to discern, since use is mostly below 2% for all groups with large confidence intervals. Non-Hispanic Black and non-Hispanic other individuals may be more likely to use pipes than the other groups.


Interactive Dashboard
Use the dashboard below to examine ever and current use of various tobacco/nicotine products by sex, age group, and race & ethnicity from 2012-2022, and to compare product use between two selected groups from 2012-2022. Note that the data for this integrated dashboard has been subset to 50,000 observations to improve performance.




Note: If a plot is not loading for a certain selection criteria, try deleting the Nicotine/Product use criteria and adding it again. This dashboard can also be found separately on this website.

title: Tobacco Use Project
text:
Conclusion and Summary
This analysis of NHIS tobacco use data from 2012 to 2022 demonstrates shifts in the prevalence of different tobacco and nicotine use patterns in the United States. While cigarette use has declined, there has been a significant rise in e-cigarette use, especially among younger adults.
Tobacco and nicotine product use is unevenly distributed across sociodemogrpahic groups. Over time, use of cigarettes, pipes, smokeless tobacco, and cigars has decreased for younger age groups, while e-cigarette use increased in these groups. Men were consistently more likely to use all tobacco and nicotine products than women. Non-Hispanic Asian and Hispanic individuals are less likely to use cigarettes, non-Hispanic Other and non-Hispanic White individuals are the most likely to use e-cigarettes, non-Hispanic Black individuals are the most likely to use cigars, non-Hispanic White and non-Hispanic other individuals are the most likely to use smokeless tobacco, and non-Hispanic Black and non-Hispanic Other individuals are more likely to use pipes.
A decline in cigarette use is promising for public health, but an increase in e-cigarette use poses a new challenge for tobacco control efforts. Targeted interventions accounting for disparities between groups is necessary to reduce tobacco-related harm and subsequent deaths. Continued monitoring of these trends is necessary as the tobacco product landscape continues to change. Future research could examine transitions between different products, dual use (or poly use) of products, and the recent emergence of nicotine pouch products.

title: About the Data
text:
The data used in this analysis come from the National Health Interview Survey (NHIS), a nationally representative, cross-sectional household survey conducted annually by the National Center for Health Statistics. Public-use NHIS data files for survey years 2012â€“2022 are from the Integrated Public Use Microdata Series (IPUMS), which harmonizes variable names and coding across survey years for longitudinal analysis. The dataset includes demographic characteristics, tobacco use variables, and complex survey design variables.

Variable Creation and Data Cleaning
Five tobacco/nicotine product categories were defined: cigarettes, e-cigarettes, cigars, smokeless tobacco, and pipes. For each product, two binary indicators were created: ever use (respondents who reported ever using the product), and current use (respondents who reported ever use and current use every day or some days). Current use of cigarettes or cigars only includes participants who used 100 or more cigarettes or cigars in their lifetime, respectively, as is standard in tobacco analyses.
Demographic variables were categorized into age groups (18â€“24, 25â€“34, 35â€“54, and 55+), sex (male or female), and race & ethnicity (Hispanic, Non-Hispanic White, Non-Hispanic Black, Non-Hispanic Asian, and Non-Hispanic Other).
To account for the NHIS complex sampling design, the following variables were used: Primary Sampling Unit (PSU), Stratification variable (STRATA), and Sampling weights (SAMPWEIGHT).These were used to construct a survey design object using the survey package to find nationally representative prevalence results.
The dataset was restricted to adults aged 18 years and older. Observations with missing values in key variables (age, sex, race/ethnicity, survey design variables, or survey year) were excluded. The cleaned dataset was saved as an RDS file for reproducibility and efficient reuse.


Data Exploration
Exploratory data analysis was conducted to assess trends in tobacco use over time across demographic groups.

Current cigarette use over time

Overall cigarette use prevalence declined rapidly over this period from almost 20% to 10%, while e-cigarette use has increased. Cigar, smokeless tobacco, and pipe use has remained relatively stable over time. The NHIS survey did not ask about e-cigarette use until 2014 and pipe and cigar use until 2015.










Current e-cigarette use by age group

Current e-cigarette use prevalence increased over time for every age group except the 55+ age group, where prevalence remained relatively steady. Prevalence of e-cigarette use is higher for each larger age group. E-cigarette use was not included in the NHIS survey until 2014.










Current cigarette use by race & ethnicity

Current cigarette use prevalence has declined over time for every race and ethnicity group. Non-Hispanic Asian and Hispanic individuals are less likely to use cigarettes than other groups. Non-Hispanic Other individuals have been the most likely to use cigarettes overall, but the prevalence of use among this age group has declined and in 2022 was similar to use prevalence in non-Hispanic White and non-Hispanic Black individuals. This group may show varying rates because of a lower sample size.











Interactive Exploration
A shiny dashboard was created for the main analysis to enable the interactive exploration of tobacco use trends across products and demographic subgroups. Users can select tobacco product types, stratify by age, sex, or race & ethnicity, and compare trends between two specifically-selected groups.


Full Workflow
See below for the data cleaning and variable creation process for the NHIS data.

# cigarette use
NHIS$smk_ever <- ifelse(NHIS$SMOKEV == 2, 1,  
                          ifelse(NHIS$SMOKEV == 1, 0, NA))
NHIS$smk_current <- ifelse(
  NHIS$SMOKEV == 2 & NHIS$SMOKFREQNOW %in% c(2, 3), 1,
  ifelse(NHIS$SMOKEV == 1 | (NHIS$SMOKEV == 2 & NHIS$SMOKFREQNOW == 1),0,NA))

# e-cigarette use
NHIS$ecig_ever <- ifelse(NHIS$ECIGEV == 2, 1, 
                             ifelse(NHIS$ECIGEV == 1, 0, NA))
NHIS$ecig_current <- ifelse(NHIS$ECIGEV == 2 & NHIS$ECIGED %in% c(1,2), 1, 
                                ifelse(NHIS$ECIGEV == 1 | 
                                         NHIS$ECIGEV == 2 & NHIS$ECIGED == 3, 0, NA))

# cigar use
NHIS$cigar_ever <- ifelse(NHIS$CIGAREV == 2, 1,
                              ifelse(NHIS$CIGAREV == 1, 0, NA))
NHIS$cigar_current <- ifelse(
  NHIS$CIGAREV == 2 & NHIS$CIGARFREQNOW %in% c(1, 2), 1,
  ifelse(
    NHIS$CIGAREV == 1 | (NHIS$CIGAREV == 2 & NHIS$CIGARFREQNOW == 3),
    0,
    NA))

# smokeless tobacco use
NHIS$smkless_ever <- ifelse(NHIS$SMKLSEV == 2, 1, 
                                ifelse(NHIS$SMKLSEV == 1, 0, NA))
NHIS$smkless_current <- ifelse(NHIS$SMKLSEV == 2 & NHIS$SMKLSFREQNOW %in% c(1,2), 1, 
                                   ifelse(NHIS$SMKLSEV == 1 | 
                                            NHIS$SMKLSEV == 2 & NHIS$SMKLSFREQNOW == 4, 0, NA))

# pipe use
NHIS$pipe_ever <- ifelse(NHIS$PIPEV == 2, 1, 
                             ifelse(NHIS$PIPEV == 1, 0, NA))
NHIS$pipe_current <- ifelse(NHIS$PIPEV == 2 & NHIS$PIPEFREQNOW %in% c(1,2), 1, 
                                ifelse(NHIS$PIPEV == 1 | 
                                         NHIS$PIPEV == 2 & NHIS$PIPEFREQNOW == 3, 0, NA))

# race & ethnicity
NHIS$race_eth <- ifelse(NHIS$HISPETH %in% c(20,60), "Hispanic",  # Hispanic
                             ifelse(NHIS$HISPETH == 10 & NHIS$RACENEW == 100, "NHW",  # non-Hispanic White
                                    ifelse(NHIS$HISPETH == 10 & NHIS$RACENEW == 200, "NHB",  # non-Hispanic Black
                                           ifelse(NHIS$HISPETH == 10 & NHIS$RACENEW == 400, "NHA",  # non-Hispanic Asian
                                                  ifelse(NHIS$HISPETH == 10 & NHIS$RACENEW %in% c(300, 510, 542), "NHO", NA)))))  # non-Hispanic Other
NHIS$race_eth <- factor(NHIS$race_eth, 
                            levels = c("Hispanic","NHA", "NHB", "NHO", "NHW"))


options(survey.lonely.psu = "adjust")

NHIS$sexcat <- dplyr::case_when(
  NHIS$SEX == 1 ~ "Male",
  NHIS$SEX == 2 ~ "Female",
  TRUE ~ NA_character_)

NHIS$agecat <- dplyr::case_when(
  NHIS$AGE >= 18 & NHIS$AGE <= 24 ~ "18â€“24",
  NHIS$AGE >= 25 & NHIS$AGE <= 34 ~ "25â€“34",
  NHIS$AGE >= 35 & NHIS$AGE <= 54 ~ "35â€“54",
  NHIS$AGE >= 55 ~ "55+",
  TRUE ~ NA_character_)

NHIS$race_eth <- dplyr::recode(
  NHIS$race_eth,
  "Hispanic" = "Hispanic",
  "NHA" = "Non-Hispanic Asian",
  "NHB" = "Non-Hispanic Black",
  "NHO" = "Non-Hispanic Other",
  "NHW" = "Non-Hispanic White",
  .default = NA_character_)

NHIS <- NHIS %>% 
  select(YEAR, agecat, sexcat, race_eth, smk_current, ecig_current,
         cigar_current, smkless_current, pipe_current,
         smk_ever, ecig_ever, cigar_ever, smkless_ever, pipe_ever,
         PSU, STRATA, SAMPWEIGHT)

NHIS <- NHIS %>%  filter(
  !is.na(YEAR),
  !is.na(agecat),
  !is.na(sexcat),
  !is.na(race_eth),
  !is.na(PSU),
  !is.na(STRATA),
  !is.na(SAMPWEIGHT))

saveRDS(NHIS, "NHIS.rds")

NHIS <- NHIS |>
  sample_n(50000)

saveRDS(NHIS, "NHIS_reduced.rds")

See below for the code used to create the interactive visualization.

NHIS <- readRDS("NHIS.rds")

sex_choices  <- sort(unique(na.omit(NHIS$sexcat)))
age_choices  <- sort(unique(na.omit(NHIS$agecat)))
race_choices <- sort(unique(na.omit(NHIS$race_eth)))

svy_design <- svydesign(
  id = ~PSU,
  strata = ~STRATA,
  weight = ~SAMPWEIGHT,
  data = NHIS,
  nest = TRUE)

product_labels <- c(
  smk_current = "Current Cigarette Use",
  smk_ever = "Ever Cigarette Use",
  ecig_current = "Current E-Cigarette Use",
  ecig_ever = "Ever E-Cigarette Use",
  cigar_current = "Current Cigar Use",
  cigar_ever = "Ever Cigar Use",
  smkless_current = "Current Smokeless Tobacco Use",
  smkless_ever = "Ever Smokeless Tobacco Use",
  pipe_current = "Current Pipe Use",
  pipe_ever = "Ever Pipe Use")

make_label <- function(product, subgroup) {
  paste0(product, ", ", subgroup)}

clean_text <- function(x) {
  x <- as.character(x)
  x <- gsub("\\(|\\)", "", x)
  x <- gsub(",\\s*1$", "", x)
  trimws(x)}

ui <- navbarPage(
  "NHIS Tobacco Dashboard",
  tabPanel("Subgroup Trends",
           sidebarLayout(
             sidebarPanel(
               selectInput("product1",
                           "Tobacco/Nicotine Product Use",
                           choices = setNames(names(product_labels), product_labels),
                           multiple = TRUE),
               selectInput("group",
                           "Stratify by",
                           choices = c(
                             "Race" = "race_eth",
                             "Sex" = "sexcat",
                             "Age" = "agecat")),
               uiOutput("subgroups_ui")),
             mainPanel(
               plotlyOutput("plot1", height = "600px")))),
  tabPanel("Group Comparison",
           sidebarLayout(
             sidebarPanel(
               selectInput("product2",
                           "Tobacco/Nicotine Product Use",
                           choices = setNames(names(product_labels), product_labels),
                           multiple = TRUE),
               h4("Group A"),
               selectInput("race_A", "Race",
                           race_choices,
                           multiple = TRUE),
               selectInput("sex_A", "Sex",
                           sex_choices,
                           multiple = TRUE),
               selectInput("age_A", "Age",
                           age_choices,
                           multiple = TRUE),
               hr(),
               h4("Group B"),
               selectInput("race_B", "Race",
                           race_choices,
                           multiple = TRUE),
               selectInput("sex_B", "Sex",
                           sex_choices,
                           multiple = TRUE),
               selectInput("age_B", "Age",
                           age_choices,
                           multiple = TRUE)),
             mainPanel(
               plotlyOutput("plot2", height = "600px")))))

server <- function(input, output) {
  output$subgroups_ui <- renderUI({
    req(input$group)
    choices <- switch(input$group,
                      race_eth = race_choices,
                      sexcat = sex_choices,
                      agecat = age_choices)
    selectInput("subgroups",
                "Select Groups",
                choices = choices,
                multiple = TRUE,
                selected = choices)})
  
  data1 <- reactive({
    req(input$product1, input$subgroups)
    group_var <- input$group
    design_sub <- subset(
      svy_design,
      get(group_var) %in% input$subgroups)
    bind_rows(lapply(input$product1, function(var) {
      df <- svyby(
        as.formula(paste0("~", var)),
        as.formula(paste0("~YEAR + ", group_var)),
        design_sub,
        svymean,
        vartype = "ci",
        na.rm = TRUE)
      
      df$YEAR <- as.numeric(as.character(df$YEAR))
      subgroup <- clean_text(as.character(df[[group_var]]))
      df <- df %>%
        mutate(
          Prevalence = .data[[var]] * 100,
          CI_Lower = ci_l * 100,
          CI_Upper = ci_u * 100,
          
          Product = product_labels[[var]],
          Subgroup = subgroup,
          
          Label = make_label(Product, Subgroup)) %>%
        mutate(Label = clean_text(Label))
      df}))
  })
  
  output$plot1 <- renderPlotly({
    df <- data1()
    ggplotly(
      ggplot(df,
             aes(x = YEAR, y = Prevalence,
                 color = Label, group = Label)) +
        
        geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
        geom_point() +
        
        geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = CI_Lower,
                        ymax = CI_Upper,
                        fill = Label),
                    alpha = 0.15,
                    color = NA,
                    show.legend = FALSE) +
        labs(x = "Year", y = "Prevalence %") +
        theme_minimal(base_size = 14))})
  
  data2 <- reactive({
    req(input$product2)
    compute_group <- function(race, sex, age) {
      design_sub <- subset(
        svy_design,
        race_eth %in% race &
          sexcat %in% sex &
          agecat %in% age)
      bind_rows(lapply(input$product2, function(var) {
        df <- svyby(
          as.formula(paste0("~", var)),
          ~YEAR,
          design_sub,
          svymean,
          vartype = "ci",
          na.rm = TRUE)
        df$YEAR <- as.numeric(as.character(df$YEAR))
        subgroup <- paste(race, sex, age, collapse = " ")
        subgroup <- clean_text(subgroup)
        
        df <- df %>%
          mutate(
            Prevalence = .data[[var]] * 100,
            CI_Lower = ci_l * 100,
            CI_Upper = ci_u * 100,
            
            Product = product_labels[[var]],
            Label = make_label(Product, subgroup)) %>%
          mutate(Label = clean_text(Label))
        df}))}
    bind_rows(
      compute_group(input$race_A, input$sex_A, input$age_A),
      compute_group(input$race_B, input$sex_B, input$age_B))
  })
  
  output$plot2 <- renderPlotly({
    
    df <- data2()
    ggplotly(
      ggplot(df,
             aes(x = YEAR, y = Prevalence,
                 color = Label, group = Label)) +
        
        geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
        geom_point() +
        
        geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = CI_Lower,
                        ymax = CI_Upper,
                        fill = Label),
                    alpha = 0.15,
                    color = NA,
                    show.legend = FALSE) +
        
        labs(x = "Year", y = "Prevalence %") +
        theme_minimal(base_size = 14))})
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Olivia Kaitlyn Roberts
text:
Download CV
Welcome to my website! I am a second year masterâ€™s student at the University of Michigan School of Public Health studying Computational Epidemiology and Systems Modeling with a graduate certificate in Complex Systems. My current area of research focuses on tobacco control, where I analyze trends in tobacco use using multistate transition modeling as part of the data analysis and modeling team for the Center for the Assessment of Tobacco Regulations (CAsToR).
I earned my undergraduate degree in Mathematics, with minors in Music and Interdisciplinary Sciences, from the University of South Dakota in 2024. During my time there, I did research on the intersection of mathematics and music, which continues to be an area of interest in my life. I am passionate about teaching mathematics and have experience working as a math tutor, a course assistant for a high school summer math camp, and an instructor assistant for a nursing statistics course.
Outside of academia, I am also a dedicated yoga practitioner and teacher; I have been teaching yoga since 2021 and have organized yoga festivals and workshops. I hold two 200-hour yoga teaching certifications and have experience teaching vinyasa, sculpt, and ashtanga yoga.
